articles.title: Labour MPs Reject Calls for Parliamentary Inquiry into Starmer's Mandelson Claims
articles.summary: Senior Labour figures have pushed back against demands for a new parliamentary investigation into whether Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer misled MPs about the vetting process for Lord Mandelson's appointment as UK ambassador to the US. Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch accused Starmer of misleading Parliament 'multiple times' and urged Labour MPs to support a Privileges Committee inquiry. However, Labour MP Dame Emily Thornberry, chair of the Foreign Affairs Committee, said there was no 'rush' for a new inquiry, suggesting some MPs were 'trying to score points ahead of the local elections'. Former Labour ministers Lord Blunkett and Alan Johnson also called an inquiry a 'waste of money'. The Commons Speaker must allow a debate, and MPs would vote on launching an investigation. Given the government's majority, a large number of Labour MPs would need to support or abstain for it to pass. The Privileges Committee previously investigated Boris Johnson for misleading Parliament over lockdown parties. Under the Ministerial Code, knowingly misleading Parliament requires resignation, while inadvertent errors must be corrected promptly. Lord Mandelson was sacked after seven months due to his friendship with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Starmer apologized for the appointment but faces ongoing questions about whether vetting was rushed. Badenoch claimed Starmer misled Parliament when he said 'full due process' was followed.

articles.title: Trump Reassures King Charles's Safety Ahead of State Visit Amid Security Concerns
articles.summary: President Donald Trump has assured that King Charles III will be 'very safe' during his upcoming state visit to the United States, following security discussions between the White House and Buckingham Palace. The reassurance comes after a gunman accessed an event attended by Trump in Washington D.C. on Saturday, during which a Secret Service agent was lightly injured and the president and first lady were rushed to safety. The King and Queen privately expressed sympathies to the Trumps after the incident. The four-day state visit will commence in Washington D.C., with the King and Queen being greeted at the White House by President Trump and First Lady Melania Trump. The UK government hopes the visit can ease diplomatic tensions between the two nations. The UK's ambassador to the US, Sir Christian Turner, emphasized that the visit is about 'renewing and revitalising a unique friendship.' The King will also visit the 9/11 Memorial in New York. Trump told the BBC he believes the visit could help repair relations with the UK. The streets around the White House are adorned with British and US flags, setting the stage for several days of political theatre and a charm offensive by the UK.

articles.title: UK's Healthy Life Expectancy Drops by Two Years
articles.summary: A report from the Health Foundation reveals that healthy life expectancy (HLE) in the UK has fallen by about two years over the past decade, now just under 61 years for both men and women. This decline places the UK among only five of the 21 richest countries where HLE has decreased, and it has the second steepest fall. The analysis, based on Office for National Statistics data from 2012-2014 and 2022-2024, highlights stark inequalities: those in the wealthiest 10% of areas enjoy about 20 more years of good health than those in the poorest 10%. For instance, Richmond, London, has an HLE of 69 for men and 70 for women, while Blackpool's men have only 51 years and Hartlepool's women 51 years. London was the only region to see improvement. The report attributes the decline to poverty, poor housing, obesity, mental ill health, and the COVID-19 pandemic. It warns that HLE is now below the state pension age (66 or 67) in over 90% of areas, affecting people's ability to work. The UK ranks 20th out of 21 rich countries, ahead of only the US. The Health Foundation calls this a watershed moment for policymakers.

articles.title: Arson Arrest After Fire Destroys LGBT+ Nightclub
articles.summary: A 51-year-old man has been arrested on suspicion of arson with intent to endanger life after a fire destroyed Pink Punters, a well-known LGBT+ nightclub in Milton Keynes, UK. The fire broke out in the early hours of Sunday morning while the club was packed with customers. Everyone was evacuated safely, and no injuries were reported. The police have urged the public not to speculate about the motive while the investigation continues. The owner, Frank McMahon, whose family has owned the club for 36 years, expressed devastation at the loss of the building but relief that no one was harmed. He promised to rebuild the club, emphasizing that it is about the community, not just the physical structure. Eyewitness Molly Firman described the chaotic evacuation, noting that staff acted quickly to get people out. The fire spread rapidly, and thousands watched from the roadside. The incident has shocked the local LGBT+ community, as Pink Punters has been a safe and welcoming venue for decades.

articles.title: Palestinian Local Elections: A Step Toward Unity?
articles.summary: Palestinian authorities declared local elections held in the Gaza town of Deir al-Balah and across the occupied West Bank a success on Sunday, calling them a step toward long-delayed presidential elections and eventual statehood. The elections, organized by the Palestinian Authority (PA), were for local councils responsible for services like water, roads, and electricity. In Deir al-Balah, turnout was 23%, attributed to challenges like displacement and outdated records. In the West Bank, turnout reached 56%, over half a million voters. Hamas, which controls parts of Gaza, did not field candidates and did not obstruct the vote. Candidates had to accept the Palestine Liberation Organization's program, which recognizes Israel and renounces armed struggle, effectively sidelining Hamas. Results showed dominance by independents and Fatah, the faction leading the PA. The election in Gaza was the first in over two decades and is seen as a symbolic pilot as the PA seeks to politically link the territories. Rami Hamdallah, head of the election commission, called holding the election in Deir al-Balah a significant achievement and expressed hope for future elections across Gaza.

articles.title: Inside the Unsolved Gardner Museum Heist
articles.summary: In 1990, two men disguised as police officers stole 13 artworks from the Isabella Stewart Gardner Museum in Boston. The heist remains the largest art theft in history, with the artworks valued at over $500 million. The thieves entered by tricking a security guard, then handcuffed the guards and cut paintings from their frames. Stolen works included Vermeer's 'The Concert' and Rembrandt's 'Christ in the Storm on the Sea of Galilee', along with pieces by Degas and Manet. A Napoleonic eagle finial was also taken. The museum has offered a $10 million reward. The FBI has identified suspects but not publicly named them. Former FBI agent Geoff Kelly wrote a book detailing the investigation and naming the men he believes responsible. The case remains open, and the empty frames still hang in the museum as a reminder.
